result.markdown: --- title: "Gradient Calculation Through Computational Graph" description: "The way, your favorite deep learning library learns" from: markdown+emoji author: - name: Koushik Khan url: https://koushikkhan.github.io/ date: 03-19-2023 categories: [deep learning] image: numbers.jpg draft: false --- # Introduction In our high school mathematics, we have learnt *Differential Calculus*, which has introduced the term 'differentiation' or 'derivative' of a function with respect to a variable. This derivative has played a significant role in today's deep learning algorithms. We all are familiar with the concept of derivative and hopefully, have solved a lot of exercises. I this post, I will revisit the core idea of computing derivativea and also try to develop the idea that is being used behind the learning process of a neural network. # Derivative of a function Let us take a simple function $f(.)$ that depends on the input $x$. The derivative of $f(x)$ w.r.t $x$ is defined as follows: $$ \frac{df}{dx} = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{(x+h) - x} $$ The animation below, made with [geogebra](https://www.geogebra.org), will make the definition easier to understand. Once you open this, you will see there are two sliders controlling: + the coordinate of the point $A$ and + the amount of change $h$ (only positive now) to put on point $A$. If you make $h$ very close to zero, you will understand how the line segment $\overline{PQ}$ gradually becomes the tangent of the curve at point $A$. So, as per the definition, the ratio $\frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{(x+h) - x}$ is nothing but the *tangent* or $tan()$ of the angle $\angle{QPP'}$ from the triangle $\triangle{QPP'}$. Theoratically, when $h$ is very close to zero, the ratio gives you the value of the tangent of the curve at the point $(x,0)$. Therefore, the overall definition of derivative of a function, geometrically refers to the tangent value of the curve $y = f(x)$ at the point $x$. The tangent of a curve at some point $x$ is important as it gives us an idea about the nature of the curve locally. Geometrically, if the tangent line makes an acute angle with the $X-Axis$ (which is the case here), then it denotes a positive change on the output and when it makes an obtuse angle, it denotes a negative change on the output. If the tangent line horizontal to the $X-Axis$, then there is no change on the output. Similar information can also be obtained by evaluating the expression of derivative at some point $x$. If the limiting value of the ratio is positive, then obviously, there is a positive change on the output otherwise the change is negative and having a value as zero indicates there is no change on the output. Note that, till now, we have discussed only on a scalar valued function with a scalar valued input. We will now try to get some ideas to calculate derivative when either of the function and input or both of them are vector valued. # Derivative of vector valued functions ## Functions of type $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ Such functions take vector (s) as input (s) and return a scalar. Loss functions like **MSE** or **Cross Entropy** can be couple of examples. In this case, derivative of $f(\vec{x})$ will also be a vector of the same dimension, as given below: $$ \frac{df}{d\vec{x}} = \left(\frac{df}{dx_1}, \ldots, \frac{df}{dx_n}\right) $$ ## Functions of type $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m$ Such functions take vector (s) as input (s) and return another vector with different dimension as output. A simple example can be a function $f: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^3$: + input: $\vec{x} = \left(x_1, x_2\right)$ and a matrix of parameters $\mathbf{W}_{3 \times 2}$ + output: $\vec{y} = \mathbf{f(\vec{x})} = \left(w_{11}x_1+w_{12}x_2, w_{21}x_1+w_{22}x_2, w_{31}x_1+w_{32}x_2)\right)$ In this case, derivative of $\mathbf{f(\vec{x})}$ will be a matrix of dimension $m \times n$, as given below: \begin{align} \frac{d\vec{y}}{d\vec{x}} &= \left(\frac{d\vec{y}}{dx_1}, \ldots, \frac{d\vec{y}}{dx_n}\right) \\ &= \begin{pmatrix} \frac{dy_1}{dx_1} & \frac{dy_1}{dx_2} & \ldots & \frac{dy_1}{dx_n} \\ \frac{dy_2}{dx_1} & \frac{dy_2}{dx_2} & \ldots & \frac{dy_2}{dx_n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \frac{dy_m}{dx_1} & \frac{dy_m}{dx_2} & \ldots & \frac{dy_m}{dx_n} \\ \end{pmatrix} \end{align} This matrix is often referred to as **Jacobian** of $\vec{y}$ with respect to $\vec{x}$. # Computational graph A *computational graph* is a directed acyclic graph that keeps track of the sequence of operations performed during the run time of an algorithm. Let us take an example to understand it. ```{mermaid} %%| fig-width: 4.5 graph LR a(a = 3) --> c(c = a + 1) --> a(a = 3) a(a = 3) --> d(d = a + b) b(b = 2) --> d(d = a + b) c(c = a + 1) --> e(e = c * d) d(d = a + b) --> e(e = c * d) ``` The above diagram is typically referred to as a computational graph, where both **a** and **b** are inputs to the graph and **e** is the output of the graph. Each of the boxes in the graph is called a *node* where a mathematical operation is performed. ```{block, type="mermaid"} classDef latex fill:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px; flowchart LR A[Square] -->|LaTeX| B((Circle)) C[Square] -->|$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-x^2}dx=\sqrt{\pi}$$| D((Circle)) classDef latex fill:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px; style latex fill:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px; window.MathJax = { tex: { inlineMath: [['$', '$'], ['\\(', '\\)']] } } ```
